pages.full_text:
packaging (although it's shown in the book as providing a machine.) The taps don't work for providing water for the van. The shower tap took ages to run warm (I counted to 70, a conventional tap) & the foot tray blocked. They were most un welcoming when we arrived.
__Saturday__
   The sprinklers were still on when we left in the morning. We drove to Arapile & Simon & Eve walked up a ridge to where Wellington stood, watching & where he had his glass. There was a gap in the French column, so he sprang an attack & decimated 40,000 French soldiers in 40 minutes. It was the beginning of the end of Napoleon's Europe. They were fighting in 1812, July 21st, only a fortnight before us. The hills are baking hot, the nights cold. No tents were issued until 1813. At the end of the battle, the stubble caught fire & burned up the wounded. Wellington had 52,000 men. The French numbered 50,000. Wellington found a good place to watch from. (He loved riding, whereas his Spanish counterpart, having once
pages.notes: The page begins mid-paragraph, continuing from the previous spread. The 'Saturday' heading is underlined. The text describes a visit to Arapile (Los Arapiles, near Salamanca, Spain), the site of the Battle of Salamanca (July 21st 1812). The diarist mentions a fortnight before the anniversary, suggesting their visit was around July 7–8. No year is explicitly written on this page; nearby spread B_0144 is dated 2008 July (a Spain trip), suggesting this is also 2008. 'July 21st' refers to the 1812 battle date, not the diary date. The iso_guess for the Saturday date is null as the year cannot be confirmed from this page alone. Simon and Eve are family members who walked up the ridge.

pages.notes: Handwritten page number '4' visible in top-right corner, written in green ink (possibly a later annotation). The page continues the Salamanca battle narrative, then contains hand-drawn diagrams illustrating the battle formations: a set of horizontal lines labelled 'line (used by English)', horizontal lines with a right-pointing arrow labelled 'column (used by French)', and a series of concentric squares labelled 'Square'. Below these diagrams is the label 'Spanish Guerrilla' next to a glued-in or printed colour illustration of a mounted Spanish guerrilla soldier with a lance/spear and red pennant, on a dark horse. The illustration is signed by the artist (appears to read 'Vela 78' or similar). The formation diagrams are not a route sketch but rather battlefield tactic illustrations.
